1.pericope_label: A Song of Trust in God
1.synthesis: Isaiah 26 opens with the song of the redeemed city: “In that day this song will be sung in the land of Judah: We have a strong city; he sets up salvation as walls and bulwarks” (26:1). The strong city contrasts with the heap to which the proud city was reduced in 25:2: the humble city that trusted in YHWH is the one that stands when the fortress of the ruthless falls. Open the gates, that the righteous nation that keeps faith may enter (26:2). The famous promise follows: “You keep him in perfect peace whose mind is stayed on you, because he trusts in you” (26:3). The shalom shalom (perfect peace / peace peace) of v. 3 is the doubled Hebrew superlative: complete, whole, undisturbed peace for the one whose mind is fixed on YHWH. Trust in YHWH forever, for in YAH, the LORD, is an everlasting rock (26:4). He has humbled those who dwell on high: the lofty city he lays low (26:5). The path of the righteous is level; you make level the way of the righteous (26:7). My soul yearns for you in the night; my spirit within me earnestly seeks you (26:9). The prayer for divine judgment follows: when the wicked see YHWH’s grace, they do not learn righteousness (26:10); though favor is shown, they still deal corruptly. The psalm closes with acknowledgment that other lords have ruled over Israel, but YHWH alone is acknowledged; they are dead and will not live (26:13–14).
1.historical_context: The “perfect peace” of v. 3 (shalom shalom) uses the intensifying repetition that Hebrew employs for superlatives; the English translation “perfect peace” or “complete peace” captures the doubled form. The rock imagery of v. 4 (tsur olamim, everlasting rock) echoes the Deuteronomic rock theology (Deut 32:4) and the Psalter’s use of YHWH as rock of refuge. The reference to other lords ruling over Israel (v. 13) may allude to the period of foreign domination or to the attraction of foreign deities; the declaration that these lords are dead (v. 14) is a polemic against the deities of the occupying powers.
1.literary_structure: The song opens with the strong city (vv. 1–2), moves to the promise of perfect peace (vv. 3–4), develops the theology of the leveled path (vv. 5–10), and closes with the acknowledgment of YHWH’s sovereignty and the deadness of other lords (vv. 11–15). The structure is city of salvation → peace of trust → judgment of pride → exclusive YHWH lordship. The perfect peace of v. 3 is the center of gravity around which the entire song orbits.
1.theological_application: The promise of 26:3—“you keep him in perfect peace whose mind is stayed on you”—is the Isaianic theological account of peace that transcends circumstances. The peace is not the absence of external threat (the song is sung in the context of cosmic judgment) but the interior stability of the one whose mind is fixed on YHWH rather than on the threats surrounding them. Communities shaped by this promise cultivate the mental discipline of “setting the mind on things above” (Col 3:2) as the practice that produces the peace that passes understanding.
1.christology: The “everlasting rock” of 26:4 (tsur olamim) is identified by Paul as Christ: “and the Rock was Christ” (1 Cor 10:4). The “perfect peace” of v. 3 finds its fullness in the peace Christ gives: “Peace I leave with you; my peace I give to you. Not as the world gives do I give to you” (John 14:27). The other lords who have died (v. 14) contrast with the one Lord who was dead and is alive forevermore (Rev 1:18): the resurrection distinguishes the Lord of the strong city from every pretender to lordship.

16.synthesis: The closing section of Isaiah 26 turns from the song of trust to a prayer that emerges from suffering: “LORD, in distress they sought you; they poured out a whispered prayer when your discipline was upon them” (26:16). The suffering has been like pregnancy without birth: “Like a pregnant woman who writhes and cries out in her pangs when she is near to giving birth, so were we because of you, O LORD; we were pregnant; we writhed; but we have given birth to wind” (26:17–18). The labor that produced nothing, the travail that yielded only wind, the inability to deliver the hoped-for salvation: this is the experience of exile and defeat. But then the great declaration: “Your dead shall live; their bodies shall rise. You who dwell in the dust, awake and sing for joy! For your dew is a dew of light, and the earth will give birth to the dead” (26:19). The resurrection of the dead is announced as YHWH’s act: his dew of light will cause the earth to give birth to those who dwell in it. The oracle closes with a summons: “Come, my people, enter your chambers, and shut your doors behind you; hide yourselves for a little while until the fury has passed by” (26:20). YHWH is coming to punish the inhabitants of the earth for their iniquity (26:21).
16.historical_context: Isaiah 26:19—“your dead shall live; their bodies shall rise”—is one of the two most explicit Old Testament declarations of bodily resurrection (along with Daniel 12:2). The “dew of light” by which the earth gives birth to the dead draws on the ancient Near Eastern association between dew and life-giving moisture; the dew of Hermon in Psalm 133:3 is associated with divine blessing and life. The summons to enter the chambers and hide (v. 20) echoes the Passover instruction to stay inside during the destroying angel’s passage (Exod 12:22); YHWH’s fury passing by will spare those inside their chambers.
16.literary_structure: The section moves from the prayer of distress (v. 16) through the failed labor analogy (vv. 17–18) through the resurrection declaration (v. 19) through the protective summons (v. 20) through the announcement of judgment (v. 21). The pivot is v. 19: the labor that produced wind (v. 18) will be replaced by the divine dew that produces resurrection. The structure is failure → divine reversal → protective hiddenness → coming judgment.
16.theological_application: The resurrection declaration of 26:19 grounds the church’s hope in a specific divine promise, not a philosophical inference: YHWH’s people will rise because he has said so. The failed labor of vv. 17–18—pregnant with hope, delivering only wind—is the pastoral diagnosis of every community that has invested deeply in hope that has not yet been fulfilled. The pivot to v. 19 is not a denial of the pain of the failed labor but the announcement that the divine dew will produce what human effort could not.
16.christology: “Your dead shall live; their bodies shall rise” (26:19) is the Old Testament text that most directly anticipates the bodily resurrection of Christ and of those who are in him. Paul’s argument in 1 Corinthians 15 draws on the same prophetic ground: Christ the firstfruits has risen, and those who belong to him will rise at his coming (1 Cor 15:22–23). The “dew of light” by which the earth gives birth to the dead is the power of the resurrection, which Paul calls “the Spirit of him who raised Jesus from the dead” (Rom 8:11): the same Spirit will give life to the mortal bodies of those who are his.
